applicationGuide.summary: Call your regional Area Agency on Aging to request a companion for your parent — no forms or income verification required.

steps.title: Find your regional agency
steps.description: Vermont has four Area Agencies on Aging. Age Well serves central Vermont, Northeast Kingdom Council serves the northeast, Senior Solutions serves the southeast, and Southwestern Vermont Council serves the southwest.

steps.title: Call the helpline
steps.description: Call 1-800-642-5119 for Age Well or Senior Solutions areas, or contact your regional agency directly to explain your parent's needs for companionship and daily living assistance.

steps.title: Assessment and matching
steps.description: The agency will assess your parent's needs and match them with a trained volunteer companion based on personality, interests, and geographic location.

steps.title: Service begins
steps.description: Your parent's companion will start with introductory visits and gradually build to the agreed-upon schedule of 10-40 hours per week as needed.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Varies by volunteer availability in your area
applicationGuide.tip: Be specific about what type of help your parent needs most — transportation, errands, companionship, or bill-paying assistance — to help with companion matching.

contacts.label: Age Well & Senior Solutions Helpline
contacts.description: Central and southeastern Vermont companion requests

contacts.label: Senior Solutions Direct
contacts.description: Southeastern Vermont office

contacts.label: Northeast Kingdom Council
contacts.description: Northeastern Vermont companion services

contacts.label: Southwestern Vermont Council
contacts.description: Southwestern Vermont companion services

applicationNotes: Companion availability varies by region — rural areas may have longer waits for volunteer matches
The program prioritizes clients who are isolated or have limited family support nearby

faqs.question: What's the difference between this program and hiring a private companion?
faqs.answer: Senior Companion volunteers provide friendship and light assistance but cannot do medical tasks, personal care, or heavy housework. Private companions cost $15-25/hour while this program is free, but volunteers have limited availability and flexible schedules rather than guaranteed hours.

faqs.question: Can my parent's companion drive them to medical appointments?
faqs.answer: Yes, companions can provide transportation and receive $0.70 per mile reimbursement for driving. However, they cannot assist with medical care or accompany your parent into medical procedures — just transportation and waiting room support.

faqs.question: How long can my parent keep the same companion?
faqs.answer: Companions can continue serving as long as both parties want to continue and the volunteer remains active in the program. Many companion relationships last for years, providing consistent friendship and support.

faqs.question: What happens if the companion and my parent don't get along?
faqs.answer: Contact your Area Agency on Aging immediately to request a different companion match. The agencies understand that personality fit is crucial and will work to find a better match based on interests, communication style, and needs.

faqs.question: Can my parent receive a companion if they already get other home services?
faqs.answer: Yes, companions can supplement other services like Meals on Wheels, home health aides, or housekeeping services. The companion focuses on friendship, errands, and light assistance that other programs may not cover.

faqs.question: How many hours per week will the companion visit?
faqs.answer: Companions typically provide 10-15 hours per week but can serve up to 40 hours depending on your parent's needs and the volunteer's availability. The schedule is flexible and arranged between your parent and their companion.

programs.name: Senior Solutions Vet-to-Vet Program
programs.tagline: Veteran volunteers provide companionship and support to senior veterans who need connection and understanding.
programs.intro:
If your parent is a veteran 60+ who feels isolated or could use regular check-ins, this program matches them with fellow veterans who understand military experience. Because the volunteers are also veterans, they share common ground that creates deeper connections than standard senior programs.

The program provides regular phone chats, safety check-ins, and emotional support tailored to veterans' unique experiences. This is purely companionship and peer support — not medical care or financial assistance. Volunteer coordinators match personalities and service backgrounds to create meaningful relationships.

Available to veterans in the 46 towns served by Senior Solutions in southeastern Vermont, including the Brattleboro area. No income limits apply — this program is available regardless of assets or income level.

summary: Live in Senior Solutions service area
otherRequirements: Must be a veteran with military service
Resident of one of the 46 towns served by Senior Solutions in southeastern Vermont

applicationGuide.summary: Call the Senior Solutions HelpLine — no formal application needed, just a conversation with the volunteer coordinator.

steps.title: Call Senior Solutions HelpLine
steps.description: Phone 800-642-5119 or 802-885-2669 to reach the volunteer coordinator. Explain that your parent is a veteran interested in the Vet-to-Vet companionship program.

steps.title: Brief phone screening
steps.description: The coordinator will ask about your parent's military background, interests, and what kind of companionship they're looking for — phone calls, visits, or both.

steps.title: Volunteer matching
steps.description: Staff will match your parent with a veteran volunteer based on service era, personality, and location. The American Legion Brattleboro Post 5 helps identify suitable volunteer matches.

steps.title: Introduction and ongoing contact
steps.description: The volunteer coordinator facilitates the initial introduction, then the veteran volunteer begins regular contact according to what works for both parties.

applicationGuide.tip: Have your parent's military service information ready — branch, years served, and any specific interests or hobbies that might help with matching.

contacts.label: Senior Solutions HelpLine
contacts.description: Primary number for Vet-to-Vet Program and volunteer coordinator

contacts.label: Senior Solutions Local
contacts.description: Local Senior Solutions office for southeastern Vermont

contacts.label: Senior Solutions Email
contacts.description: General email for Senior Solutions services

applicationNotes: This is companionship only — volunteers are not trained caregivers and don't provide medical or personal care assistance
Matching may take time depending on volunteer availability and compatibility, but the coordinator will keep you updated on progress
relatedPrograms: Senior Solutions Veteran Directed Care Program

faqs.question: Can my parent get this program if they live outside the Brattleboro area?
faqs.answer: Yes, as long as they live in one of the 46 towns served by Senior Solutions in southeastern Vermont. The program isn't limited to Brattleboro — that's just where the American Legion partnership is based. Call 800-642-5119 to confirm your parent's town is covered.

faqs.question: What if my parent has never talked about their military service?
faqs.answer: That's common, and veteran volunteers understand this. Many older veterans didn't discuss their service much, but connecting with someone who shared that experience can be meaningful. The volunteer coordinator matches based on comfort level and personality, not just military details.

faqs.question: How often will the volunteer contact my parent?
faqs.answer: This varies based on what both people want and the volunteer's availability. Some pairs talk weekly by phone, others might visit monthly or call every few days. The volunteer coordinator helps establish expectations that work for everyone involved.

faqs.question: Can my parent participate in this and still get other Senior Solutions services?
faqs.answer: Yes, this program works alongside other Senior Solutions offerings like home care, transportation, or meals programs. The Vet-to-Vet companionship is just one piece of what Senior Solutions provides to veterans and other seniors in their service area.

faqs.question: What happens if the volunteer match doesn't work out?
faqs.answer: The volunteer coordinator can arrange a different match. Personality fit matters for meaningful companionship, so staff expect that some pairings need adjustment. There's no penalty for requesting a different volunteer match.

faqs.question: Does my parent need to prove their veteran status?
faqs.answer: The program description doesn't specify documentation requirements, but having basic military service information (branch, years served) ready when you call will help with volunteer matching. The coordinator can tell you if any formal proof is needed.

stateOverview.intro: Vermont offers 15 programs specifically designed to help seniors stay independent and reduce family caregiving costs. These range from comprehensive medical care through PACE to monthly grocery assistance through 3SquaresVT for Older Vermonters. Many programs have simplified applications for seniors — 3SquaresVT requires renewal only every 3 years, and some Medicare cost-sharing programs have no asset limits. Start by checking the programs that provide the biggest financial impact: Medicaid coverage, Medicare cost assistance, and home care services that prevent costly nursing home placement.

startHere.name: Medicaid for Aged, Blind, and Disabled (ABD)
startHere.why: Comprehensive health coverage that can save thousands annually if your parent qualifies based on age 65+ and limited income.

startHere.name: Qualified Medicare Beneficiary (QMB), Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary (SLMB), Qualified Individual (QI)
startHere.why: Covers Medicare premiums and medical costs for parents earning less than $1,781/month as a single person.

startHere.name: Choices for Care (CFC) Waiver
startHere.why: Provides home care services to prevent nursing home placement for seniors who need daily help with bathing, dressing, or medications.

startHere.name: 3SquaresVT for Older Vermonters
startHere.why: Monthly grocery assistance with a simplified 3-year application for seniors 60+ living on Social Security.

programs: Medicaid for Aged, Blind, and Disabled (ABD)
Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
Qualified Medicare Beneficiary (QMB), Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary (SLMB), Qualified Individual (QI)
byNeed.description: Comprehensive health coverage and Medicare cost assistance for qualifying seniors.

byNeed.need: Home care and daily living support
byNeed.description: Services that help your parent stay safely at home instead of moving to a nursing facility.
Monthly grocery benefits and help reducing heating costs through bill assistance and home improvements.

programs: Legal Assistance for Seniors (Vermont Legal Aid Elder Law Project)
byNeed.description: Free resources to help you navigate caregiving, understand benefits, and resolve problems.
Volunteer programs providing social connection and peer support for isolated seniors.
Part-time paid training positions for unemployed adults 55+ earning minimum wage while building job skills.

quickFacts: Vermont uses simplified applications for seniors — 3SquaresVT requires renewal only every 3 years instead of annually
Medicare Savings Programs in Vermont have no asset limits, only income limits of $1,781/month for single person
Weatherization assistance can provide up to $10,000 in free home energy improvements to reduce heating costs
PACE serves Vermont seniors through OneCare Vermont and provides all medical and social services under one program
stateOverview.resourcesVsBenefits: Vermont offers both income-qualified benefits and free resources for all seniors. Benefits like 3SquaresVT, Medicaid ABD, and Choices for Care require your parent to meet specific income limits and provide monthly assistance. Free resources like SHIP Medicare counseling, Legal Assistance for Seniors, and the Long-Term Care Ombudsman are available regardless of income — these help you maximize other benefits and resolve problems with care.
